introHtml: Git workflow questions assess day-to-day professional habits. Common topics include rebasing vs merging, cherry-pick, conflict resolution, and branching strategies like trunk-based development or GitFlow. Knowing how to recover from mistakes — reflog, reset, revert — is a strong signal of experience.

topics.bodyHtml:
Merge vs Rebase

# Merge: creates merge commit, preserves history
git checkout main
git merge feature/login  # merge commit created, diverge is visible in log

# Rebase: moves feature commits to tip of target, linear history
git checkout feature/login
git rebase main  # replays feature commits on top of current main

# Golden Rule of Rebase:
# NEVER rebase public/shared branches (rewrites history = new commit SHAs)
# Safe: rebase local feature branches before creating PR

# Interactive rebase: rewrite last N commits
git rebase -i HEAD~4
# pick   = use commit as-is
# reword = change commit message
# edit   = pause to amend commit (git commit --amend, then git rebase --continue)
# squash = combine with previous commit (keep both messages)
# fixup  = combine, discard this commit's message
# drop   = remove commit entirely

Useful Commands

# Stash: temporarily save uncommitted work
git stash push -m "WIP: login feature"
git stash list                   # list all stashes
git stash pop                    # apply latest and remove
git stash apply stash@{2}        # apply specific, keep in stash
git stash drop stash@{0}         # delete specific stash

# Cherry-pick: apply specific commit(s) to current branch
git cherry-pick abc123
git cherry-pick abc123^..def456  # range (inclusive)

# Bisect: binary search for bug-introducing commit
git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD              # current commit is bad
git bisect good v1.2.0           # last known good tag
# Git checks out middle commit, you test, then:
git bisect good  # or bad
# Repeat until Git reports the first bad commit
git bisect reset

# Reflog: safety net — all HEAD movements
git reflog
git checkout HEAD@{5}            # restore to 5 HEAD moves ago
git reset --hard HEAD@{3}        # undo to 3 reflog entries ago

Branching Strategies

StrategyDescriptionBest for
Git Flowmain + develop + feature/ + release/ + hotfix/ branchesScheduled releases, enterprise, mobile apps
Trunk-basedShort-lived branches, frequent merges to main, feature flagsCI/CD, fast-moving teams, SaaS
GitHub Flowmain + feature branches via PRsWeb apps, continuous deployment

Conventional Commits

# Format: type(scope): description
feat(auth): add OAuth2 login with Google
fix(api): handle null user in profile endpoint
docs(readme): update local setup instructions
refactor(db): extract connection pool to module
test(user): add integration tests for registration
perf(feed): use cursor pagination for news feed
ci(github): add Playwright E2E to CI workflow
chore(deps): upgrade express to 5.0

# Breaking change: add ! or BREAKING CHANGE in footer
feat!: remove deprecated v1 API endpoints
# or:
feat(api): new auth system
BREAKING CHANGE: Remove /auth/login, use /auth/token instead

Semantic Versioning

MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH example: 2.1.3
PATCH (2.1.x): backwards-compatible bug fixes
MINOR (2.x.0): backwards-compatible new features
MAJOR (x.0.0): breaking changes
Pre-release: 1.0.0-alpha.1, 2.0.0-rc.3
Build metadata: 1.0.0+20240301

items.question: What is the difference between the working tree, the index, and the commit history?
items.answerHtml: The working tree is your current filesystem state. The index, or staging area, is the snapshot you are preparing for the next commit. Commit history is the series of immutable snapshots already recorded in the repository.
items.flashcardBackHtml: The working tree is what you are editing now; the index is what will go into the next commit.

items.question: How do merge and rebase differ conceptually?
items.answerHtml: Merge preserves the actual branch history by creating a merge commit. Rebase rewrites commits so they appear on top of a new base, producing a cleaner linear history but changing commit identities. The practical tradeoff is history clarity versus history preservation.
items.flashcardBackHtml: Merge preserves branch topology; rebase rewrites commits onto a new base for a linear history.

items.question: Why is force pushing dangerous on shared branches?
items.answerHtml: It rewrites remote history and can discard or orphan teammates' commits if they built on the previous state. It is safest only when everyone understands the workflow and the branch is intentionally disposable.

items.question: You made three messy commits locally and want one clean commit before opening a PR. What options do you have?
items.answerHtml: You can do an interactive rebase to squash or reorder commits before pushing, or create a new branch and cherry-pick the clean changes. The key is to rewrite local history before it becomes shared remote history.

items.question: A merge conflict appears in a file you barely touched. How would you reason about resolving it safely?
items.answerHtml: I would understand both sides of the conflict semantically, not just choose ours or theirs blindly. The safe path is to read the nearby code, preserve both required changes, run tests if possible, and avoid treating conflict markers as a mechanical text problem.

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: git rebase rewrites history (new commit hashes); never rebase commits that others have already pulled
git reflog is the safety net for almost any mistake — commits are not permanently deleted for 30 days
Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, chore:) enable automated changelogs and semantic versioning
git bisect performs binary search through history to find the commit that introduced a bug
Trunk-based development with feature flags reduces merge conflicts and keeps the main branch always releasable
PR reviews should focus on design and correctness; style is enforced by linters, not humans
